upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very shallow, stable boundary layer with a surface temp of 13.6°C and an extraordinary inversion: 850mb temp is 16.8°C, meaning the atmosphere is actually warming with altitude — a strong subsidence inversion capping thermals well below 1500m MSL. The surface-to-850mb lapse rate of -2.12°C/km is far below dry adiabatic (9.8) and even moist adiabatic (6.5), confirming strongly suppressed convection and essentially no usable thermal development today. Wind shear is notable with 700mb flow from 230° at 10 kts and 500mb from 230° at 25 kts, but near-surface flow is nearly calm (2 kts at 50°), indicating a decoupled, light surface layer beneath the inversion.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area/SF region. METARs largely benign: KSFO reporting calm-to-light winds, high broken layer at 20,000ft, temp 25°C — unusually warm for late March, consistent with subsidence inversion. KHAF showing 320° at 10 kts with haze and 6-7SM visibility — marine air present but not deep. KRHV reporting 300-310° at 3-5 kts with SCT080 (8,000ft scattered) and 27°C — warm, stable inland conditions. KAPC 230° at 8 kts, 26°C. KWVI 260° at 7 kts, 27°C. No precipitation, no significant turbulence advisories. Warm temps across all inland sites confirm strong subsidence; no convective hazards.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, NW-facing launch, thermal and ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 is primarily a thermal site that benefits from valley heating generating NW afternoon sea breeze flow up the face. Ideal winds are NW to W at 8-15 kts with a healthy thermal boundary layer reaching well above launch elevation.
sites.todaySetup: The dominant story at Ed Levin 1750 today is a strong subsidence inversion confirmed by the KOAK sounding that caps thermals far below useful altitudes, combined with highly variable and predominantly light winds throughout the day. The morning window shows NNE winds at 8-10 kts at 10m (HRRR 09-11Z), which is cross to slightly off the NW-facing launch, with heavy cloud cover (99% at 08Z collapsing by 11Z) suppressing heating. Wind80m reads 8-16 kts from the NE-ENE early, well above surface speeds, indicating shear and residual gradient flow aloft. By midday the surface flow becomes nearly calm and shifts erratically — 3.4 kts from 347° at 13Z, dropping to 1-2 kts — with the boundary layer only reaching 490-525m above ground, which is barely above launch elevation at 1750ft (~535m). Cloud cover pulses between 0% and 99% in short intervals throughout the day, suggesting broken convective cloud remnants without actual thermal development. Afternoon sees light NNE to N flow at 5-6 kts, but with a BL height capped at 520m and the sounding showing no thermal potential, this is a day of light, variable, unflyable conditions at the high launch. KRHV confirms warm stable air at 27°C with only 300-310° at 3-5 kts at the valley floor.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Heavy overcast at 08Z clearing partially by 11Z; NNE winds 8-10 kts at surface but 80m winds still 9-16 kts from NE — cross to launch and sheared. BL only 65-340m. Not viable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds collapse to near-calm (3-5 kts) and become highly variable in direction (347°, 16°, then back); cloud cover oscillates wildly 0-99%. BL reaches 490-525m but thermals suppressed by inversion. Unflyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light NNE/N flow 5-6 kts, BL plateaus near 525m then decays after 17Z. No thermal trigger, no ridge lift at these speeds. Winds too light and variable for any useful soaring.

verdict.hazards: Strong subsidence inversion caps BL near launch elevation; highly variable wind direction; no thermal development; cross/off-direction flow most of day

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — mid-hill launch, thermal training hill, NW-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft launch is a training hill used for short sledder flights and beginner thermalling when conditions align. It needs light-to-moderate NW flow and a functioning BL to be useful; it's sheltered enough that off-direction flow can create rotor or unflyable conditions quickly.
sites.todaySetup: The 600ft launch is a no-fly today for the same fundamental reason as the 1750: the subsidence inversion crushes any thermal development, and surface winds are either too strong/cross in the morning or too light and variable through the afternoon. Early morning sees 15 kts from NNE at surface (HRRR 08Z) — far too strong for a training hill — tapering to 6 kts by 10Z but still from NNE/NE which is cross to a NW-facing site. By midday winds drop to 1-4 kts from WSW to W, which is actually opposite to what this site needs, creating potential rotor from the back side. BL heights at 600ft elevation are marginally above launch (435-585m above ground level per model) but the inversion is so strong there is zero thermal climb potential. The afternoon W/NW flow at 3-6 kts is too light to soar and the BL collapses after 18Z.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 15 kts NNE at 08Z — too strong and cross for a training hill. Dropping to 6 kts by 10Z but still NNE/NE cross. Not viable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds go nearly calm then shift WSW to W at 1-4 kts — opposite to NW launch face. Rotor risk from reversed flow. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light NW 3-6 kts developing after 16Z but too light for any soaring. No thermal support. Sledder-only at absolute best, and only for an experienced instructor assessment on site.

verdict.wind: 1-15 kts variable, mostly cross or light NW late
verdict.hazards: Morning winds too strong and cross; midday reversed/rotor-prone flow; afternoon too light; inversion kills all thermal development

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — beginner training hill, multiple aspects, sheltered valley
sites.howItWorks: The 300ft bunny hill is used for ground handling and short training flights. It is the most wind-forgiving of the Ed Levin launches and needs only light, steady, on-heading flow (typically NW) and ideally a small thermal trigger or ridge effect to stay up beyond a sledder.
sites.todaySetup: Today at the 300ft hill is marginal at best for pure sledder training flights in the afternoon window, and even then conditions are messier than ideal. The core problem is the same subsidence inversion and light variable winds, but at this low elevation the relevant question is simply whether ground-level wind is light and on-heading enough for basic training. Morning shows 8 kts NNE at 08Z (too strong for beginners, cross) tapering to light ESE/S by 09-10Z. Through midday, winds are 4-6 kts from WSW to SW (230-255°), which is a usable direction for some aspects of this hill but gusty relative to mean speed at times. Afternoon (15-18Z) sees WNW flow at 5-6 kts — this is the most reasonable window for training flights under instructor supervision. However, KRHV confirms 27°C and SCT080, meaning thermals are theoretically possible but the inversion means any bubble will be weak and broken, creating punchy, variable surface conditions inappropriate for true beginners without close instructor oversight.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 8 kts NNE at 08Z — cross and too strong for beginner hill. Quickly goes light and variable S/SE by 09-10Z. Not useful.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: WSW 4-6 kts, somewhat usable direction. But erratic shifts and weak broken thermals make surface conditions punchy and unpredictable. Instructor supervision essential.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: WNW 5-6 kts 15-18Z is the best training window — lightest and most consistent flow of the day. Suitable for supervised sledder flights only; no soaring expected.

verdict.hazards: Variable direction early; weak broken thermals create punchy surface layer despite light mean winds; inversion suppresses any climb; beginners need close instructor supervision

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W-facing ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site that needs steady W to NW onshore flow at 10-20+ kts to generate lift along the coastal bluff. The marine layer and sea breeze drive the lift; the site is unusable in offshore flow or when marine layer is too deep to allow a clean sea-breeze punch.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock has a genuinely complex and mostly unflyable setup today. The critical issue through midday is wind direction: HRRR shows 10m winds from ESE to E (90-130°) from 08-14Z — this is offshore/cross flow at a W-facing site, meaning zero ridge lift and potential rotor behind the bluff. The buoys confirm NW flow at 9.7 kts offshore (SF Bar 46026, HMB 46012 both 310° at ~10 kts), indicating the NW sea breeze exists offshore but hasn't yet punched through to the immediate coastal face. KHAF at 20Z (approximately 13:00 local) is reporting NW 10 kts, 20°C, clear — the marine air is arriving at Half Moon Bay but the directional data at Mussel Rock itself shows a confusing transition. The HRRR model shows a shift to NW/NNW by 15Z (332° at 3.3 kts surface, then 325° at 6.8 kts by 16Z), suggesting the sea breeze front arrives around 15-16Z (8-9 AM local... wait — these are UTC hours, so 15Z = 8 AM PDT, 16Z = 9 AM PDT). Re-evaluating: at 15Z (8 AM local) winds go 332° at 3.3 kts — very light NNW, not enough for soaring. By 16Z (9 AM local) 325° at 6.8 kts and 17Z 320° at 6.6 kts — NW at 7 kts, marginal for soaring. The morning hours (08-14Z / 1-7 AM local) show ESE flow — overnight offshore drainage that should have fully transitioned by mid-morning local time. By late morning local time (18-19Z), winds are 295-270° at 5-7 kts but 80m winds are 7-9 kts showing the sea breeze is building aloft. SST at 12.6°C (HMB buoy) is cold, supporting a moderate marine layer, but KHAF is already clear and 20°C suggesting the immediate coastal zone has cleared. Overall, Mussel Rock is a marginal late-morning to afternoon site today with 6-8 kts NW — usable for P3 pilots but light for reliable soaring.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Through roughly 7 AM local (14Z UTC) winds are ESE to E — offshore/cross, rotor-prone, zero ridge lift. The NW sea breeze transition arrives around 8-9 AM local (15-16Z) at only 3-7 kts. Too light and transitional for safe flight early.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: By 11 AM local (18Z UTC) NW flow at 5-7 kts surface, 7-8 kts at 80m — marginal soaring possible along the bluff for experienced pilots. Conditions are light but direction is correct. KHAF confirms NW 10 kts with clear skies.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Flow remains NW at 5-7 kts into the evening. Light but consistent ridge lift possible for skilled pilots. Wave period of 10s from HMB buoy suggests organized swell not disrupting low-level flow significantly.

verdict.hazards: Offshore ESE flow through mid-morning creates rotor risk; light NW sea breeze when it arrives is borderline for reliable soaring; cold SST 12.6°C supports lingering marine influence; wind transition timing uncertain

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland peak/ridge, NW-facing, thermal and ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak soars on a combination of thermals triggered by inland heating and NW ridge flow funneled through the Fremont gap. Ideal conditions are 8-15 kts NW with a healthy BL reaching well above the 2500ft summit. It shares the same valley breeze cycle as Ed Levin.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the exact same fatal flaw as Ed Levin: the KOAK sounding's powerful subsidence inversion completely suppresses thermal development, and surface winds are light and variable throughout the day. Morning winds (08-12Z / 1-5 AM local) show NNE to NE flow at 5-10 kts with 80m winds 8-16 kts from the same direction — this is cross/off the NW launch face and driven by overnight gradient, not a sea breeze. BL heights are only 65-340m in the morning, far below the ~750m MSL summit elevation. As the day progresses, winds collapse to near-calm at 13Z (6 AM local) then slowly rebuild from N to NNW at 5-8 kts during afternoon hours. The BL reaches 490-525m above ground, which is marginal relative to launch but the inversion (confirmed by the dramatically inverted temperature profile at 850mb — 16.8°C vs surface 13.6°C) means any parcel rising from the valley floor is immediately capped. Wind120m data at Mission Peak shows increasing upper-level flow (4-9 kts) from the W-NW in the afternoon, but this is decoupled from the surface. KRHV at 27°C with SCT080 confirms warm, thermally-suppressed conditions. No viable flying window exists today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE to NE winds 5-10 kts — cross to NW launch face; 80m winds 9-16 kts indicating shear. BL only 65-340m, well below summit. Overcast early. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds go near-calm (3.4 kts at 347°) then light and variable around N (5 kts at 16°). BL reaches ~500m but inversion kills thermals. Cloud cover oscillates 0-99% suggesting unstable but capped airmass. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNW to N flow 5-6 kts, BL plateaus ~525m then decays. No thermal or ridge soaring possible at these speeds and with this inversion. Not flyable.

verdict.hazards: Subsidence inversion caps BL near or below summit elevation; no thermal development; cross/off-heading flow all day; wind shear between surface and 80m layer

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday looks considerably better. GFS/HRRR show light SE to calm flow morning transitioning to W-WNW 5-7 kts by afternoon with BL heights reaching 425-435m. Tomorrow.io shows light winds with gusts only to 6 kts. The inversion should be less intense with clearing skies and more heating potential. Marginal thermal day — possible sledder-to-soaring window in the afternoon if valley heating fires. Winds remain very light so thermals may be weak and cycle-dependent.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday morning starts with light S-SE flow (3-4 kts), transitioning to SW then W-WNW afternoon. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 4-5 kts only. BL reaches 320-435m. A potential light thermal training day in the afternoon (14-16Z local 7-9 AM... note UTC offset: 14Z = 7 AM PDT, so afternoon local = 19-23Z). Best window likely 12-15Z local (early-mid afternoon PDT). Light but usable for supervised training.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar to 600ft. Light SW to W flow 3-8 kts afternoon with gusts only 4-5 kts per Tomorrow.io. BL reaches 260-420m. Better than today for basic training flights. Afternoon window usable under instruction.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday shows a significant wind increase at Mussel Rock: HRRR forecasts WSW 8-12 kts at surface with 80m winds 9-14 kts, all from 243-265° (W to WSW). Tomorrow.io confirms 8-12 kts WSW with gusts to 12 kts and — critically — fog/marine layer with visibility dropping to 0.1km by midday. This is a whiteout fog scenario. Despite the wind strength being flyable, near-zero visibility in marine fog makes this a NO FLY for safety. Watch for late afternoon clearing possibility if fog lifts, but confidence low.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday shows very light winds — near calm at midday (0.8 kts from 166° at 12Z) shifting to W-WNW 3-5 kts afternoon. Tomorrow.io shows light SW winds gusting only to 8 kts. Clear skies (0% cloud most of day) and BL reaching 425-435m. Better thermal potential than today but still quite light. A possible marginal thermal day for patient pilots in the early-mid afternoon window.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Sunday (March 29) shows overcast conditions (100% cloud cover 12-14Z and 88% at 18Z) with W-WNW winds building to 6-8 kts by afternoon and BL heights reaching 750-825m — the best BL of the 4-day period. However, 100% cloud cover through midday will suppress thermal triggering. A possible late afternoon soaring window if clouds break (26% cloud at 16Z is promising) with 7-8 kts W-NW. Monitor cloud evolution.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Sunday shows continued W-WNW flow at 4-8 kts with 100% overcast all day. Not a viable ridge soaring day under full cloud cover. Winds are in range but visibility and lift quality under overcast are poor.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Sunday has 100% overcast through the day with light W-WNW flow at 4-5 kts surface but notable 80m winds reaching 7-10 kts. BL heights are healthy (585-905m) but cloud cover kills thermals. Watch for any afternoon break — 19% cloud at 16Z suggests possible partial clearing late.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Monday (March 30) shows 100% overcast all day with precipitation probability reaching 2-6%. BL heights are actually the highest of the period (up to 1255m at 14Z) with W-NW winds 4-10 kts, but rain risk and full overcast make this unflyable. System approaching.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Monday shows W to WSW winds 4-10 kts with 92-100% cloud cover and precip probability 3-8%. A frontal or pre-frontal system is moving through. Not flyable.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Monday is a washout — 100% overcast, W-NW winds 4-8 kts at surface but 80m winds 9-12 kts, precip 2-6%, BL up to 1010m. High BL under a closed overcast with rain risk. Do not fly.

watchlist: Mussel Rock Saturday: Tomorrow.io shows near-zero visibility (0.1km) in marine fog despite good wind speeds — confirm fog situation before driving out
Subsidence inversion today is exceptionally strong (850mb temp 16.8°C vs surface 13.6°C) — thermals completely suppressed at all inland sites
Mussel Rock morning ESE offshore flow (through ~8 AM local) creates rotor risk behind the bluff — do not launch until confirmed NW sea breeze established on site
Sunday-Monday cloud/precip system: BL heights increase dramatically Sun-Mon but overcast and rain risk make the weekend's best BL heights unusable — plan accordingly
KHAF haze (6-7SM) this afternoon may signal residual marine layer moisture that could affect Mussel Rock burn-off timing tomorrow
tldr: Today is essentially a no-fly day across the board — a powerful subsidence inversion (850mb warmer than the surface) kills thermals at all inland sites, and Mussel Rock's morning offshore flow transitions to only marginal 6-8 kts NW by late morning. If you really need to get in the air, Mussel Rock has the best shot with a late-morning NW sea breeze window for P3+ pilots, or the Ed Levin 300ft hill for supervised P2 training sledders in the afternoon. Tomorrow (Saturday) looks more interesting at Mission Peak and Ed Levin if thermals fire, but watch out for Mussel Rock being fogged out.
